summary: Gruber reviews the WWDC 2016 keynote, covering announcements across WatchOS 3, tvOS, the renamed MacOS, iOS 10, iMessage, and Swift Playgrounds. He praises the WatchOS 3 navigation overhaul as a needed conceptual simplification, and characterizes 'common sense prevails' as the theme of the event. He highlights iOS 10's practical improvements and its surprising anti-lock-in moves, like removable system apps and third-party app integration at the OS level. He argues iMessage is vastly underappreciated as a platform and sees Apple turning it into something far more ambitious with extension APIs.
key_points: WatchOS 3 represents the thorough UI reinvention Apple Watch needed, replacing Glances with a simpler Dock concept and reducing common tasks to one or two taps
iOS 10 is characterized as the 'anti-lock-in release' — removable system apps, third-party VoIP on the lock screen, and per-contact default messaging apps
'Common sense prevails' is the unifying theme: tvOS now allows games to require dedicated controllers, reversing a policy that was holding back the platform
iMessage is being transformed from a messaging app into a genuine platform through extension APIs, moving beyond plain text URLs to exchangeable software objects
Swift Playgrounds is Apple's most approachable programming environment since HyperCard, potentially a first step toward Xcode on iPad
money_quotes: A conceptual simplification, along with a deliberate effort to reduce many common tasks to just one or two taps, is just what the doctor ordered for Apple Watch.
As for the purported dramatic improvements to app launching times and background data refreshing, I'll believe it when I see it.
"Common sense prevails" is arguably the theme of this year's announcements.
iOS 10 looks like the anti-lock-in release.
I've been arguing for a while now that iMessage is vastly under-appreciated as one of the most popular and best messaging platforms in the world.

One way to think about it is as an effort to move away from sharing plain text (and often ugly, unreadable) URLs that open in Safari and instead exchange software "objects" that are usable right there in the message thread.
This is the most approachable programming environment from Apple since HyperCard.
key_insight: WWDC 2016's announcements are unified by a theme of common-sense corrections — simplifying WatchOS, loosening iOS lock-in, and elevating iMessage into a platform — showing Apple listening to criticism and making practical improvements over flashy ones.
